excerpt_en: June 13th — So, as I wrote in detail in yesterday's post, my base of operations has changed dramatically. I'm now living in a castle! Yes, a literal "castle." This majestic building behind me is my new home and
body_en:
June 13th

So, as I wrote in detail in yesterday's post,

my base of operations has changed dramatically.

I'm now living in a castle!

Yes, a literal "castle." This majestic building behind me is my new home and the stage for my activities.

A New Stage of Challenge: Charleville Castle

I had been based in Dublin, Ireland, but through a wonderful connection, I was given the opportunity for a new challenge. The place is Charleville Castle, about an hour and a half by train from Dublin. I'll be staying here as a live-in volunteer.

I don't actually sleep in the castle itself, but in a main house-like building on the grounds. From there, I commute to the castle every day to carry out various activities.

This Charleville Castle isn't just any old castle. It's a "Grade A" listed building recognized for its extremely high historical value — one of only 2% of buildings in all of Ireland to receive this designation, making it an incredibly rare and famous structure.

Its value is highly regarded across all of Europe as well.

However, the reality of this castle is far from the glamorous tourist attraction you might imagine. Currently, it doesn't actively welcome regular tourists. Instead, it mainly provides its majestic spaces as filming locations for movies and TV dramas. Apparently, it has even been used as a location for Hollywood films.

Because of this, apart from the spaces used for filming, honestly, very little maintenance has been done. Of course, the castle owner's family lives here, but accepting volunteers like me is something that hasn't happened since the pandemic.

This time, I was fortunate enough to receive permission to stay as the first Japanese person to live in this castle as a volunteer.

My Mission on Day One

So, volunteering in this dream-like environment. The first mission I was given was surprisingly simple.

"First, get used to the castle's environment."

And then, a more practical challenge that came with it.

That was: "Create a livable environment with your own hands."

Today marks the start of my second morning at the castle.

The first day flew by with greetings, a tour of the castle, and shopping for daily necessities. Starting today, I'm getting down to the real work of setting up my own living foundation.

Unfortunately, this is far from the "elegant life in an old castle" that you might be picturing.

To put it bluntly, it's incredibly dirty (lol).

Historical value and comfortable living conditions are two completely separate things. This isn't the castle's fault — it's simply the result of the place not being maintained for the purpose of "living in."

That's exactly why my challenge starts here.

In this wonderful environment, I'm going to build — with my own hands, starting today — a foundation where I, as a Japanese person, can live comfortably and healthily.

As a first step, I decided to start by cleaning the bathroom that I've been given for my personal use.

VS The Bathroom: Is This Really the 21st Century?

Now, let me show you the battlefield I'm about to take on. This is the bathroom I've been assigned. It's about one or two tatami mats in size. The moment I opened the door, I couldn't help but gasp.

The first thing I noticed was the toilet. It was, to put it kindly, not clean. Next, the bathtub. Various items had been piled in here, covered in a thick layer of dust, with mold visible here and there. And then the sink. It was in a similar state — honestly, I couldn't bring myself to wash my face or brush my teeth there.

It might look a bit cleaner in videos or photos, but there's nothing but room for improvement.

It reminded me of an abandoned "akiya" (vacant house) in Japan. It seemed like nobody had used it recently — spider webs were strung up everywhere, and it had become a little paradise for insects. I never dreamed that I'd come to a Grade A castle in Ireland and start with work that resembles renovating a vacant house in Japan.

But I'm not at all pessimistic about this situation. If anything, I'm actually a bit excited.

If I don't clean this bathroom today, I'll go two days in a row without a bath. That's something I definitely want to avoid.

Start cleaning first thing in the morning, scrub it thoroughly, let it dry, and be ready for a comfortable shower tonight. That's today's absolute goal.

Armed with the cleaning supplies and brushes I bought during yesterday's shopping trip, it's time to start cleaning.

First up, the bathtub. Since the surroundings are made of wood, I can't just splash water around recklessly.

There's no shower curtain either, so I proceed carefully yet boldly to remove the grime. Hardened dirt, stubborn mold — I scrub it all furiously with the brush.

As I worked away silently, a fundamental worry suddenly crossed my mind: "Will there even be hot water?"

I turned the faucet and waited for a while...

With a "click," warm water started flowing out.

"Hot water came out...! Thank goodness...!"

I couldn't help but exclaim out loud.

Just this alone made me deeply appreciate the blessings of modern civilization. Now I can take a shower.

All that's left is to turn this place into a space where I can truly relax. Getting hot water suddenly boosted my motivation.

Next, the sink. Since it's white ceramic, the dirt is very visible. On the flip side, that means the more I clean it, the more clearly I can see the results. I sorted through abandoned shampoos and soaps that belonged to nobody, sprayed cleaner, and polished it meticulously.

Honestly, I'm no cleaning professional. My only experience is cleaning my own home, so I have no idea if I'm doing it the right way.

But right now, that doesn't matter. The important thing is to get it clean to a level where I can use it comfortably. Since I'm the one who'll be using it, I just need to keep going until I'm satisfied. Thinking about it that way, I found a surprising surge of energy.

And then, the biggest challenge — the toilet. I had braced myself for this being the dirtiest part.

I just went into a zone. Using a powerful toilet cleaner, I scrubbed every corner with the brush

. Knowing that this is a place I'll be using every day from now on, I couldn't cut any corners. Fortunately, they had prepared a brand-new toilet seat cover for my stay.

Why Am I Cleaning?

As I cleaned away in silence, the thought "What am I doing this for?" crossed my mind.

First and foremost, it's "for myself." A clean environment is directly linked to quality of life and, by extension, mental well-being. Since I have the privilege of living in this wonderful castle in Ireland, I want to enjoy the environment to the fullest.

To do that, creating a space where I feel comfortable is essential. I simply can't allow myself to live in a dirty place.

But that's not all.

By cleaning this place up, it should also benefit future volunteers who might come after me. Right now it's just me, but by building a comfortable living foundation here, perhaps this castle can more easily accept volunteers in the future.

If my efforts contribute even a little to revitalizing this castle, nothing would make me happier.

What starts as something for myself ends up coming full circle to help someone else. Knowing that gives me extra drive.

Also, I feel that experiences like this will surely nourish my future self. Many of my friends have renovated houses through DIY projects or built their own lifestyles from scratch, and I've always admired that.

Just yesterday, I lit a wood-burning stove and split firewood for the first time in my life. The feeling of building your life with your own hands — something you'd never experience in the convenience of city living.

This is undoubtedly a precious experience that will enrich the rest of my life.

I never imagined the place where I'd put this into practice would be an old castle in Ireland, but I have a feeling this experience will come in handy someday — whether in Japan or in another country.

The Next Battlefield, and Hope

After several hours of battling, the bathroom was transformed beyond recognition. The bathtub and sink had regained their whiteness, and the toilet was now safe to use. I was filled with a sense of accomplishment and relief.

From tonight, I can enjoy a refreshing shower and reset my day.

But there's no time to rest.

The battle to secure my territory continues.

Next, I headed to the shared kitchen. This was another formidable opponent.

The area around the sink was especially bad — unidentifiable stuff had accumulated, and honestly, it smelled like a gutter. Maybe this is part of the castle's history, but as a place where I'm going to cook, it's unacceptable.

I rolled up my sleeves and started cleaning again.

I washed up all the piled dishes, scrubbed the sink, and removed the grease around the stove.

Using the heat from the wood-burning stove to dry wet cloths, I threw myself into the work.

And after the struggle, the sink had regained its sparkling shine.

Friday, Saturday, Sunday. For these three days,

I had decided to keep online work to a minimum and focus entirely on getting my lifeline in order at this castle.

Today was the first of those days, Friday. The big cleanup that started in the morning left me exhausted, but gave me an even greater sense of fulfillment.

Real life at a national treasure-class castle in Ireland.

It's gritty, it's tough, but it's an incredibly exciting challenge. My story at this castle has only just begun. I can't wait to see what each day will bring.

I'll keep sharing the real stories from this place.

excerpt_en: June 14th — Today's post is a short one. What I've been doing hasn't changed since arriving at the castle — cleaning up. Yesterday I mainly focused on the bathroom area and kitchen, but today it's the shared room used by volunteers
body_en:
June 14th

Today's post is a short one.

What I've been doing hasn't changed since arriving at the castle —

cleaning up.

Yesterday I mainly focused on tidying up the bathroom area and kitchen,

but today it's the shared room used by volunteers.

That said, I'm the only volunteer here, so I'm doing it all by myself.

The Jaw-Dropping Mess — What Caused It?

The shared spaces of the castle where I'm staying were so cluttered there was barely room to walk.

The kitchen had a mountain of used dishes piled up, and the volunteer room was overflowing with stuff.

"Is this just how things are around here?" I couldn't help but wonder.

However, the caretaker then told me something surprising. Apparently, a movie had been filmed at this castle right up until just before I arrived.

A large number of film staff and crew had been using these shared spaces, and in the midst of their hectic schedules, they kept leaving things out without time to clean up. It all piled up, and what I walked into was the "messiest it had ever been."

Once I knew the reason, the state of things in front of me made perfect sense. This wasn't someone intentionally making a mess — it was a byproduct of a busy production set.

For a Comfortable Life: A 3-Day Deep Clean

Regardless of the reason,

I'll be living here for over a month. I felt that cleaning this place up was essential for comfortable daily life. So I spent three days completely absorbed in cleaning.

Washing mountains of dishes, tidying up scattered rooms, restoring the space to its original beauty. It was hard work, but the time spent building my own living foundation with my own hands was oddly fulfilling.

Making the Most of the Free Time: On to New Challenges

Since there was no traveling during this period, I was fortunate to have plenty of time on my hands.

Alongside the cleaning, I decided to spend time on AI and online activities — something I'd been interested in for a while.

This new life started from a tough situation, but by pushing through it and investing the time that opened up into self-improvement,

I'm convinced this experience will make my life richer going forward.

First, get your surroundings in order.

Then, on to new challenges.

My life at the castle

has now truly begun.

title_en: "Castle Life" Is Like a Survival Game
excerpt_en: June 15th — My new life at a castle in Ireland. It's a far cry from the conveniences of everyday life — more like a survival game. Heating by hand: life with a wood-burning stove. This castle where I live obviously doesn't have
body_en:
June 15th

My new life at a castle in Ireland.

It's a far cry from the conveniences of everyday life —

more like a survival game.

Heating by Hand: Life with a Wood-Burning Stove

This castle where I live obviously doesn't have air conditioning. To warm up a room, we need to feed logs into the wood-burning stove and light the fire ourselves.

For now, there's still a stock of firewood, but in another week or two, it'll probably run out.

When that happens, I might have to grab an axe and start by chopping down trees in the forest.

Limited Cooking Equipment and a Far-Off Supermarket

Food is essential for "survival."

But cooking here is no simple task either.

The kitchen has a frying pan, sure,

but there's no gas stove, and not even a way to light a fire.

The only thing I can use is a toaster oven. Every day is a challenge to come up with ways to prepare meals with these limited resources.

On top of that, since the castle is deep in the woods in the countryside, even grocery shopping is a major event. The nearest supermarket is a 40-minute walk one way.

On this day, I bought groceries, detergent, and other daily necessities all at once,

but the walk back with all those heavy bags nearly tore my arms off. My body was completely wrecked, but this is going to become just another part of daily life here, I guess lol

Enjoying the Benefits of Inconvenience.

A Priceless Experience Only Possible Here

This isn't something just anyone living in Ireland can experience. It's a life unlike anything I could have known without coming to this castle.

I usually work online, but since I don't have any commuting, I spend that time preparing firewood, carrying heavy loads over long distances, and getting physical exercise. Digital work and primal physical labor.

This balance feels incredibly valuable and meaningful to me right now.

There's a lot that's tough, but I can even enjoy the inconveniences. That kind of special daily life is

what I'm living right now.

Well, it's getting cold again today.

Time to start by lighting

the stove.

title_en: A Walk Through the Forest and Big Ambitions
excerpt_en: June 16th — It's been 5 days since I started living at the castle in Ireland. The castle where I'm staying is surrounded by a vast forest. Today the weather was great, a perfect day for a walk. I finished work and firewood collection in the morning, and after lunch
body_en:
June 16th

It's been 5 days since I started living at the castle in Ireland.

The castle where I'm staying is surrounded by a vast forest.

Today the weather was great, a perfect day for a walk.

I finished work and firewood collection in the morning,

and after lunch, I ventured into the forest for an afternoon stroll.

The weather had been bad recently, so I hadn't been able to walk through the forest much — making this a precious opportunity. With volunteer work about to ramp up, I wanted to soak in the atmosphere of this world while I still could.

Drawing Inspiration for a Story in the Forest

Actually, there's another reason I'm walking through this forest.

For about three years now, I've been writing a story centered around a character called "KUKU."

And I have a grand ambition to one day turn that story into an animated film.

People might say it's "impossible," but

I'm dead serious.

Forests and nature are at the core of this story's world. That's why I wanted to actually immerse myself in a forest overseas and experience what I'd feel. With virtually no man-made objects around and surrounded by silence, this environment is the ultimate stage for focusing on writing.

While thinking about all this and walking for about 15 minutes, a massive fallen tree appeared in front of me, completely blocking the path.

This is nature in its truest form.

It's a sight you'd rarely encounter in Japan.

I'm grateful to be able to experience a world like this, and it reaffirms the importance of taking on challenges while I'm still young and physically capable.

In my 40s or 50s, it might be difficult to push through pathless trails like this.

New Paths Born from Failure

Switching topics a bit —

it's been 10 months since I came to Ireland.

My original plan was to collaborate with major Irish brands like whiskey and beer companies to expand "FOMUS," the brand I run.

But honestly,

I haven't achieved a single one of those goals yet.

This is entirely due to my own lack of skill and overly optimistic estimates. A foreigner with little track record, approaching companies with "let's collaborate" without offering any clear benefits — of course nobody was going to take that seriously.

Realizing that I couldn't keep going like this,

I changed my approach to "first, contribute to Ireland."

And that's how I ended up joining this international volunteer program at the castle.

Through this work, I want to build trust with the people of Ireland, and from that foundation, share my dreams and activities.

With about a month and a half left on my working holiday visa, I want to produce some kind of result.

Securing this volunteer position at the castle may not be a corporate achievement, but it's a huge personal step forward for me as MaSU, and it's given me confidence.

Bringing a Story to the World: A Challenge That Starts Here

A rural town in Ireland, not even Dublin.

A Japanese person living in and working at a castle there.

I believe this one-of-a-kind experience itself will enrich my story. I want to pour my life into my narrative and create something grand.

First, over this month, I'll solidify the plot of my novel and get it into a form where I can present the world I want to create to investors.

Then, in addition to my own investment, I'll seek funding from like-minded supporters and make the film a reality, delivering it to the world.

You probably think that's overly optimistic.

But life is more interesting when you take on challenges that seem reckless. I figure I might as well aim for the very top.

All of you reading this video or text — you're witnesses to my dream.

With the inspiration I've gained from this forest,

I'll pour myself into creating this story.

PS

At the end of the video, a local runner came by. The fallen tree I struggled so hard to climb over? They effortlessly took a "shortcut" around it. There are always unexpected solutions to things. Another lesson learned.

excerpt_en: June 17th — Kitchen deep clean: making the windows sparkle! Today I want to tackle the big window in the kitchen. The window frames are clogged with dust, debris, spider webs, and all sorts of grime. It's been bugging me for a while, so
body_en:
June 17th

Kitchen deep clean: making the windows sparkle!

Today I want to tackle

the big window in the kitchen.

The window frames are clogged with dust, debris, spider webs, and all sorts of grime.

It's been bugging me for a while, so

today I'm going to clean this area thoroughly.

I started with the left side first.

Then I moved on to clean the right side.

This side also had tons of spider webs and was pretty dirty. Since this window is right in front of the kitchen sink, whenever I'd set down groceries here, the filth would catch my eye, and it always bothered me.

Getting this clean will not only look better, but should also improve air circulation, so I'm going all in on this cleanup.

Cleaning complete!

The window frames look so much better now.

There's so much dust that I wore glasses while working — at times like these, glasses are way more practical than contact lenses.

Lastly, I wiped off the stubborn grime stuck on the window glass.

And with that, the entire window is clean!

For now,

I think it's at an acceptable level.

That wraps up the kitchen window cleaning for today.

Life Without Travel Creates Time

Life at this castle doesn't involve any travel.

For the past six months, I've been on the move, meeting tons of people and soaking up input, but for the next month, I plan to hole up in this castle and dedicate my time to creative work.

AI consulting, producing an animated film for a character I've created — I can now fully secure the creative time that used to get eaten up by travel. Gradually getting my life in order, then facing my own work.

My "castle life" has just begun.

There are plenty of challenges, but I want to turn each experience into strength and steadily move forward.

title_en: The Real Life After One Week of Moving In
excerpt_en: June 18th — It's been exactly one week since I started living in the castle in Ireland. Right now, I'm basically just living an ordinary life here at the castle. The first step of volunteer work was "getting the living environment in order." This week,
body_en:
June 18th

It's been exactly one week since I started living in the castle in Ireland.

Right now, I'm basically just living

an ordinary life here at the castle.

The first step of volunteer work was

"getting the living environment in order."

This week, my mission as a volunteer was to first set up my own living environment. I spent my time on unglamorous tasks like cleaning the castle's kitchen, bathroom, and toilet, and airing out the stuffy rooms.

If you asked me, "Did you do anything amazing?" — honestly, maybe not so much.

From Meeting People to Facing Myself

When I'm not cleaning, I've been working on creative projects, taking advantage of this castle's wonderful environment.

For about 11 months from June 2024, I was constantly on the move to meet as many people as possible. Going to various places to work, shoot content — it was a whirlwind of activity.

Then in mid-June 2025, I came to live at a castle in a quiet town called Tullamore in Ireland.

The castle is surrounded by forest, in a place rich with nature. I really love places like this.

The Perfect Environment to Focus on Creative Work

I don't have a car or a bicycle, so getting to town means walking about 3 to 4 km.

This September, I have a crazy event called the **"Medoc Marathon" coming up — where you run 42 km while drinking wine — so I've been running every day as training for that.

In town, you hardly see any Japanese people, let alone Asians, and my opportunities to meet people have dropped significantly. On the other hand, tourists do stop by the castle from time to time, so there are still chances to interact.

At 30 years old, I've never had a life with this much free time. Over the past year, I spent so much time meeting people that I feel I didn't have enough time to face myself.

Immersed in Novel Writing with AI

Right now, I'm writing a novel for the character project "KUKU" to promote the masu brand "FOMUS" that I run.

It's a grand story I've been developing for three years, and I eventually want to turn it into anime, movies, and manga.

I'm also using cutting-edge AI technology in my writing. By combining my original drafts with AI, I'm diving deep into the creative process to make the work even better.

The Perfect Environment for Creative Work

Until now, I didn't have a permanent home, so there were always people around me and it was hard to focus on work.

But now, the environment is absolutely perfect for creative work.

With fewer social interactions, I've been able to devote all that time to studying AI and creative activities.

I see this period as a time to hunker down and build a solid foundation. To achieve my big goal of spreading the brand worldwide, I'll use June and July to prepare thoroughly at the castle, then resume full-scale activities starting in August. Right now, it's the** "crouching" phase.

My first week at the castle was a period of enriching my own life — carrying firewood, cleaning, and the like.

Starting this week, I want to find ways I can actively contribute to the castle on my own initiative.

I'm having a great time.

Until next time.

excerpt_en: June 19th — Today I headed into town for the first time in a while! I've been getting used to life in Tullamore. Since I'm mostly at the castle, my interactions with people have decreased, but every now and then I venture into town to explore. A local hair salon
body_en:
June 19th

Today I headed into town for the first time in a while!

I've been getting used to life in Tullamore.

Since I'm mostly at the castle,

my interactions with people have decreased,

but every now and then I venture into town to explore.

Taking On a Haircut at a Local Salon

Today I went into town to get a haircut.

My hair had grown out to the point where it was covering my ears, and the volume was getting out of hand, so I wanted to freshen up. The temperature in Ireland has been climbing to around 28 degrees, and the hot days keep coming.

Tullamore is a local-feeling town with hardly any Asians — mostly Irish locals. To blend into this town, I figured I'd try going to a local hair salon.

I'm hoping that chatting with the hairdresser will be a chance to gather information about Tullamore and connect with the locals.

I've had my hair cut overseas before — in the Philippines, Poland, and Dublin — but I'm curious to see what kind of hairstyle I'll end up with this time.

I don't expect a perfect haircut,

but since I'll be in Tullamore for a while,

I'm fine with whatever style I get.

Results: Haircut AND Eyebrows!

How much did it cost?

My only request was to cut the sides short, and it turned out exactly as I imagined — nice and fresh. I'm really happy with it.

That said, it was just clippers and scissors,

so it's a bit uneven here and there lol

As for the price — just 20 euros!

Getting a cut at a Japanese salon in Dublin costs 80 to 110 euros, so this was a great deal.

The hairdresser was Irish, and we had a great time chatting about pubs, the castle, and all sorts of things.

On top of that, while getting my hair cut, I asked, "Could you trim my eyebrows too?" and they happily agreed.

That was an extra 7 euros, so the total came to 27 euros (about 4,700 yen).

In Dublin, just a cut alone costs over 10,000 yen, so this was quite the bargain.

Now that my hair is nice and tidy,

I should be able to handle the physical labor ahead more comfortably.

Enjoying a Day Off in Tullamore

After the salon, I explored the town of Tullamore.

First I headed to Tullamore Library, a place I'd been curious about.

Nothing particularly noteworthy, though lol

In the week since I started living at the castle,

I haven't gone into town except to shop at the supermarket, and I haven't had a single drink of alcohol.

For me, that's very unusual.

Thanks to that, I'm feeling fantastic lol

I only have one month in Tullamore, so I'm always thinking about how to make the most of this limited time.

After getting back to the castle,

I played around with the new video generation feature that was added to the AI image generator Midjourney.

It looks like it could be a serious asset for business,

so I'm really excited about what's to come!

excerpt_en: June 20th — Today, a new mission as a volunteer began. Up until now, I'd been working on things like cleaning the castle to set up my living environment, but this time I was given a specific mission. Restoring a trailer home for international volunteers
body_en:
June 20th

Today, a new mission as a volunteer began. Up until now, I'd been working on things like cleaning the castle to set up my living environment, but this time I was given a specific mission.

Restoring a Trailer Home for International Volunteers

The mission I was given is to clean and restore a mobile home (trailer house) for international volunteers, located about a 2-minute walk from the castle.

The trailer home has been unused since the pandemic and is quite dirty.

Inside, belongings, clothes, beds, and other items have been left behind, leaving it in a rough state. On top of that, a massive amount of lumber is scattered around the trailer, making it hard to even walk.

Right now, I'm the only international volunteer,

but new volunteers may arrive from summer through autumn.

My mission is to get this place in shape so that future arrivals can stay comfortably.

Picking Up an Axe: My First Real Physical Labor

The caretaker asked me to dismantle all the scattered lumber. Since the castle needs fuel for the wood-burning stove, my job is to chop the lumber into firewood with an axe and carry it to the castle.

Pure physical labor.

Up until now, I'd been working online and doing photography — a life completely removed from demanding physical work. I never imagined I'd be swinging an axe here.

But I don't mind physical activity, and I'd been wanting to get in shape, so I see this as a great opportunity.

Learning to Use an Axe with Help from AI

Actually, I'd never swung an axe before. I'm starting from absolute beginner level. At first, things weren't going well at all, so — believe it or not — I asked ChatGPT how to swing an axe. I took the advice like "try swinging it this way" and have been experimenting and practicing on my own.

I'm gradually getting the hang of it, but I still don't quite understand how to apply force properly, and I've already gotten blisters on my left hand.

I'm hoping that after a few more sessions, I'll be able to swing the axe much more efficiently.

What's Next

Starting today, I'm getting serious about the trailer home restoration. Outside of that, I'll continue spending my time studying AI.

I want to keep taking on

interesting challenges.

excerpt_en: June 21st — Today I continued working on restoring the trailer home. I did online work in the morning, then headed outside again in the afternoon to swing the axe and clear the lumber. There's a huge amount of wood, so it won't be done in a day
body_en:
June 21st

Today I continued working on restoring the trailer home. I did online work in the morning, then headed outside again in the afternoon to swing the axe and clear the lumber.

There's a huge amount of wood, so it won't be done in a day — I'm spreading the work out over several days.

Extreme Physical Labor! The Mission of Moving Weights

While I was working with the axe, the castle's caretaker asked me, "Could you give me a hand with something?"

He wanted to place weights on top of the trailer home, so the two of us carried these incredibly heavy, long bar-like objects together.

Then we had to lift those heavy things up onto the trailer home roof — my body was screaming from the sheer intensity of the physical labor.

Luckily, there was one more person to help out, so we managed to get the weights up there through teamwork.

It was tough work, but since I hadn't had many chances to work alongside the caretaker before, it was a good experience, and I really felt I was contributing as a volunteer.

Today was a full day of swinging the axe and hauling heavy loads — nothing but physical labor. This is the kind of experience you can only have living in a castle, so it's pretty fun in its own way.

Microwave Breaks Down! Trying to Cook on the Wood Stove

When I got back to the volunteer room after finishing work, something shocking had happened.

The microwave had stopped working.

Until now, I'd been using the microwave and electric kettle in the volunteer room's kitchen.

But with the microwave broken, all I have left is the electric kettle. I can still make instant ramen, but anything else is off the table now.

I'd been making pasta by combining the electric kettle and microwave too, so that's no longer an option.

So I came up with an idea:

warm things up using the wood-burning stove.

I had bought some frozen paella,

and I was about to give up on it since the microwave was dead. But then I placed a metal pan-like container on top of the wood stove, put the frozen paella in it, and used the heat from the fire to warm it up.

After about 20 minutes,

it was properly heated and totally edible.

Castle Life Puts Your Survival Skills to the Test

Doing physical labor outside, then warming up meals not in a microwave but on a wood-burning stove — my life has basically turned into a survival scenario.

Even if you live in Ireland, I doubt many people get to have experiences like this.

Life at this castle is definitely sharpening my survival skills. At the same time, I'm keenly aware of the incredible pace of AI evolution, so I'm also pouring myself into studying that.

Physical labor and AI studies —

activities that seem like polar opposites, running in parallel.

It makes for very fulfilling days.

title_en: The Small Downsides of Castle Life
excerpt_en: June 22nd — Another day of working outside. The trailer home restoration continues — swinging the axe and breaking down wood every day. Over the past year or so, I was doing something different every day, so I never ran out of blog material. But now that I've settled in one place
body_en:
June 22nd

Another day of working outside.

The trailer home restoration continues — swinging the axe and breaking down wood every day.

Over the past year or so, I was doing something different every day, so I never ran out of material for blog posts.

But now that I've settled in one place and am doing similar activities every day, I'm starting to feel like I'm running low on blog topics.

The "50/50" Life and the Output Dilemma

Right now, I'm living a "50/50" life —

split between international volunteer work and online work.

The volunteer activities are basically the same thing every day — swinging an axe, cleaning, and so on.

The lessons and input I can draw from these experiences

pretty much run dry after a day or two.

If it goes on longer than that, it just becomes repetitive, and I worry it might stop being interesting for readers.

For example, even if I wrote in detail about how to swing an axe,

it's too specialized, and not exactly a topic that grabs everyone's attention.

I feel like it wouldn't really benefit either me or my readers.

Balancing Specialized Learning and the Blog

The same goes for my AI studies.

AI knowledge contributes to my own growth, but if I write too much specialized content on the blog, it could create a disconnect with readers.

I share specialized AI information through the online community I run,

but writing about how amazing AI is every single day on this blog doesn't feel right. I see this blog as a place to share daily learnings and experiences, which makes it hard to write overly technical content — and that's the dilemma I'm caught in.

Today, once again, I spent the whole day swinging the axe, hauling wood, and turning it into fuel for the wood-burning stove.

I'm starting to feel like I need to come up with something new before I run out of things to write about from castle life.

A Year Where Every Day Was a Challenge

This is actually the first time in a while I've had this worry.

Over the past year, I was tackling something new every day, always engaged in different activities, so I never ran out of things to write about. I traveled to various countries and had all kinds of experiences.

But now that I've settled in one place,

living a life where my only real expense is food, I'm starting to feel like I'm running out of things to write about.

Starting tomorrow, I'll put some thought into shaking things up.
